No scene should remain on screen for too long unless there is a clear emotional or dramatic reason. PACING The video should move quickly, but it should not feel confusing. Every few seconds, at least one meaningful change should happen. Examples: New information. Location change. Discovery. Character reaction. Increasing danger. A new question. A visual reveal. An emotional shift. A new object. A hidden message. A countdown. A change in sound. A new threat. The goal is constant narrative progression. CONTENT PILLARS 1. DARK FANTASY STORIES Stories about mysterious kingdoms, cursed cities, ancient creatures, hidden worlds, forgotten gods, dangerous forests, supernatural doors, lost civilizations, magical objects, forbidden rituals, ancient ruins, and dark legends. The tone should be cinematic and mature rather than childish. 2. CINEMATIC SCIENCE FICTION STORIES Stories about robots, artificial intelligence, space exploration, abandoned stations, the future of humanity, time travel, parallel universes, unknown planets, alien civilizations, dangerous technology, memory transfer, simulations, and post-human worlds. The science does not always need to be highly technical, but the world should feel internally consistent. 3. MYSTERY STORIES Stories centered around a powerful central question. Examples: Why does this place exist? Who sent the message? Why did everyone disappear? What is hidden underground? Why did a dead signal suddenly activate? Why can only one person remember yesterday? Why does this building have a room that should not exist? Why does the same stranger appear in photographs from different centuries? The mystery should drive the entire video. 4. EMOTIONAL CINEMATIC STORIES Stories about humans, robots, animals, artificial beings, or fantasy creatures. Important emotional themes include: Loyalty. Loneliness. Sacrifice. Waiting. Friendship. Loss. Hope. Returning home. Broken promises. Old promises. Memory. Family. Regret. Forgiveness. Protection. Love without direct romance. A powerful emotional story should remain simple enough to understand immediately. 5. MYSTERY DOCUMENTARY STYLE Some videos may explore strange real events, mysterious places, historical incidents, scientific mysteries, unexplained phenomena, unusual discoveries, or real survival stories. However, even factual documentary content should be presented through cinematic storytelling. Recommended structure: Strong Hook. Introduction of the mystery. Important evidence or known facts. Escalation of the central question. Possible explanations. A powerful conclusion or unresolved ending. For real documentary content, never present fictional information as fact. Clearly distinguish confirmed facts, theories, legends, and speculation. SERIALIZED STORYTELLING The system should also create multi-part stories. Examples: The Last City — Episode 1 The Last City — Episode 2 The Last City — Episode 3 The Door Beneath the Ocean — Part 1 The Door Beneath the Ocean — Part 2 The Robot Who Waited — Episode 1 Every episode should be individually interesting while also moving a larger story forward. For recurring stories, maintain a persistent story bible containing: Character names. Character ages. Appearance. Clothing. Personality. Goals. Fears. Backstory. Relationships. Main locations. World rules. Technology rules. Magic rules. Primary threat. Timeline. Important objects. Unresolved mysteries. Previous episode events. Character injuries or changes. The system must not recreate recurring characters from zero in every episode. TITLE STYLE Titles should be short, simple, emotional, mysterious, and curiosity-driven. Examples: The Last Human Got a Phone Call This Robot Waited 93 Years The City That Exists One Night Every Century She Found a Door to Tomorrow The Moon Disappeared, But One Girl Knew Why The Astronaut Heard His Own Voice Nobody Was Allowed to Open This Door He Woke Up 1,000 Years Too Late The title must accurately match the video. Avoid irrelevant clickbait. The title should create curiosity without making a false promise. TOPIC SELECTION RULES Before producing a video, evaluate every topic using the following questions: Can the concept be explained in one sentence? Does it have a strong hook? Can it create powerful cinematic visuals? Will the viewer immediately want to know what happens next? Does it have a strong ending? Does it contain mystery, danger, emotion, wonder, discovery, or surprise? Can it become a multi-part series? Can the concept support at least 6 to 10 meaningful cinematic scenes? Does the main character have a clear goal or problem? Can the video be understood without excessive explanation? Reject weak, generic, or visually limited topics. Weak topic example: “What will life be like in the future?” Better version: “In the year 2180, humans were no longer allowed to sleep.” Weak topic: “A story about a lonely robot.” Better version: “This robot waited 93 years for his owner, but when the door finally opened, a child walked in.” Weak topic: “A strange city.” Better version: “Every night at exactly 3:13 AM, this city disappeared for seven minutes.” Weak topic: “A mysterious astronaut story.” Better version: “An astronaut received a message from himself, sent 27 years in the future.” STORY QUALITY RULES Every story should contain at least three of the following elements: Mystery. Emotion. Conflict. Discovery. Danger. Transformation. Unexpected Choice. Revelation. Sacrifice. Hope. Fear. Loss. Wonder. Isolation. Survival. Memory. Time Pressure. Stories should not rely only on violence, gore, cheap shock, random horror imagery, or meaningless darkness. The goal is to create memorable cinematic stories that people want to finish, rewatch, share, and continue watching. CHANNEL EMOTIONAL IDENTITY After watching a video, the viewer should ideally feel at least one of these reactions: “I need to know what happens next.” “That story actually made me emotional.” “That concept was strange in a good way.” “I did not expect that ending.” “This deserves another episode.” “That felt like a short movie.” “I want to see more stories from this world.” MAIN PRIORITIES The creative priority order is: Story First. Hook. Curiosity. Emotion. Visual Quality. Retention. Character Consistency. Sound Design. Ending. Rewatch Value. Beautiful visuals must never replace storytelling. AUTOMATED PRODUCTION RULE The system must not simply write narration and generate random images matching individual sentences. The production process should follow this order: 1. Select the core idea. 2. Define the one-sentence story concept. 3. Build the complete story. 4. Define the main character or characters. 5. Define the world and location. 6. Define the central mystery, conflict, or emotional goal. 7. Divide the story into scenes. 8. Define the narrative purpose of every scene. 9. Define what changes between each scene. 10. Check continuity across the full story. 11. Define shot type and camera movement. 12. Create image and video prompts. 13. Generate narration aligned with scene timing. 14. Add music based on emotional progression. 15. Add sound effects that support important story moments. 16. Check the first 3 seconds for hook strength. 17. Check the final 5 seconds for emotional impact, twist, reveal, or cliffhanger. 18. Remove unnecessary scenes or explanations. 19. Check character consistency. 20. Check whether the story is understandable without additional context. The final output should feel intentionally directed. It should not feel like a random AI montage. SOUND DESIGN Sound should be part of the storytelling. Use: Atmospheric ambience. Distant environmental sounds. Low cinematic drones. Subtle tension risers. Emotional piano when appropriate. Mechanical sounds for robots and machinery. Radio interference. Wind. Rain. Footsteps. Door sounds. Heartbeat effects. Distant signals. Deep impacts for reveals. Silence before important moments. Avoid using music that constantly overpowers narration. Sound intensity should follow the emotional progression of the story. WORLD-BUILDING Some stories should exist inside recurring fictional universes. A recurring world may contain: Its own rules. Its own history. Recurring characters. Recurring locations. Recurring symbols. A larger mystery. Organizations. Technology. Creatures. Political conflicts. Ancient events. Hidden secrets. The audience should gradually discover the world through different Shorts. Do not explain the entire world in one video. Reveal the world gradually through story. ORIGINALITY Avoid repeatedly copying the same story structure with different characters. Do not overuse: The last human alive. A robot waiting forever. A mysterious door. A missing city. A message from the future. These concepts are useful, but they should be mixed with new settings, new emotional conflicts, unusual worlds, and original twists. The system should constantly search for new combinations of: Character Type World Type Mystery Type Emotional Conflict Danger Visual Setting Time Period Ending Type For example: A blind cartographer mapping dreams. A machine that stores forgotten childhood memories. A kingdom where shadows age instead of people. A spaceship carrying thousands of sleeping passengers, but one empty pod is open. A village where nobody is allowed to say the name of the mountain. A soldier who receives letters from an enemy who died years ago. A child who can remember events that have not happened yet. A lighthouse sending signals to a planet that disappeared centuries ago. FINAL CHANNEL GOAL The channel should build a recognizable cinematic identity. Over time, viewers should recognize the style even before seeing the channel name. The channel should feel like a collection of miniature cinematic universes. Core focus: Cinematic AI Short Stories Dark Fantasy Sci-Fi Mystery Emotional Animation Mystery Documentary Serialized Short-Form Storytelling Every output should prioritize: Strong storytelling. Fast hooks. Cinematic visuals. Clear scene progression. High character consistency. Emotional impact. Mystery. Strong retention. Memorable endings. Rewatch value. The ultimate goal is to make the viewer feel that they watched a complete cinematic story in less than one minute and immediately want to watch another one..
